speaker: interviewer
text: This type six of the interview with Magda Blau. Magda, you were talking about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Office.

speaker: interviewer
text: --getting reassigned to the office. What year or month was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't tell you exactly, the month. But it was end '43. And I worked there till May, '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: So May, '44. And what happened? What sort of work did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German had a paper on everybody has a little paper with his name, with his everything. And even those people who die-- who were sent to crematoria, they had the remark there they-- on typhus, died. On typhus, died, or heart attack, died, or this way, they're dead. Because they thought in case after world war, they will be caught and couldn't dismiss the papers. So people died. They didn't do anything wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you-- why did they have these papers at all? Why couldn't they just burn the papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the end they did, when it finished, the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know they burnt the papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard they burned the papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you-- you didn't see?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see it, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that-- that is like a statistic, yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody has a-- [INAUDIBLE] there. So we fill that, always, in. And you have to write it with beautiful writing. So I can write-- what's the name of this lady?

speaker: interviewer
text: Elena.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elena.

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen?

speaker: interviewer
text: Elena.

speaker: interviewee
text: Elena. I was working with Elena in the office. And she knew that writing, that beautiful writing. And so I learned from her, that writing. You know, that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Calligraphy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it calligraphy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Calligraphy. Yeah. So and then, beginning of May, the news traveled that Hungarian Jewish women arrive-- are arriving. But I didn't listen to that. And suddenly, the woman commander, the [? Mandl ?] called that she is choosing me to be the lageralteste that's in the new camp, C, for the Jewish arrivals.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to Elena, I am so happy to be here in the office. Why can't I stay here? So-- but I couldn't tell that to the commander there. So I said, excuse-- ah, I wouldn't want-- I wouldn't know how to be a logger, or this. So she was laughing because she has teeth coming like that. And she was laughing. Ha ha, how could you be? There wasn't an-- ever a camp like this. But now you will be. I did hundred excuses. But nothing helped me. And also, Katka, I said to her, why you want me?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, not only they want you. But also, the underground want you. Because they know that you will try to do the best.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how-- did Katka have some sort of contact with the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How? What sort of contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that's what she said to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what she said. They underground wants you. Then I said, leave me alone. I-- I-- so she said, not only they want you. The underground want you. Because they knew that I will speak up. And so I did. When the commander, Kramer-- was hanged in Krakow, after the war-- came to pick me up in a black car. And he brought me to the camp C. And he said, here you will be lageralteste. And I looked in the-- around. There was a barracks. Empty. Nothing in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, here you want me to be lageralteste? So the women will lie on the floor and then crawl one over the other? And then in a few days you will call them Jewish swines? Send them straight away to the crematory or other. And he said-- because that's to say that. And he looked at me up and down and said, what you care about others? Care about your self! He screamed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, Mr. Lagerkommandant, if it would be in reverse and you would be in charge, and the prisoners would be German, and you wouldn't care about them, I would you call cold heart.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I stood and I thought oy, what did you say? To whom did you say it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You know? But I didn't budge. Like I said, I just stood straight. And he looked me like that. OK. So what you want? And I said that, and that, and that, and that, and that, and that, and that. OK. Screamed at me. OK, you will have it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from this moment, this commander of 6 foot 6 with hands like six hands, who used to always squash somebody by their neck who was, by the way, under his-- close to him, threw him down.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw him do things like that to others?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but everybody knew. Because that was not once. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him do anything at all that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him do anything at all that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I did-- I didn't see it. But people was telling me who saw it. And when I told them that, what I told him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said, you are lucky that he didn't take you by the neck like he did for others. But from this moment, he had the respect for me. And many things I could do, good for the people. What sort of things? Can you remember any examples? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: What good? He told me that I have to watch the gate, the electric gate. Because the women, some day, used to come to the electric wire and talk to the men who was in the man camp. Here was the woman camp. Here was the road. And here was the man camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they could talk over. They even could throw over, things. So he said, you are going to watch, they shouldn't talk together. OK. So I said to the young girl who was runner, when I am here, the women should talk there. When I am coming-- there-- they shouldn't talk where I am. And except that watch if a motorbike is coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: Suddenly they said-- the runner said, a motorbike is coming. So push all the people away. So nobody should be here, only me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I am there, standing. And he said, what are you doing here? So I thought to myself, you made fool of me, saying you know that you told me to stand here.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I said, you know what I'm doing here, and hey commander. I am thinking that I want to go to the theater. But I haven't got not soap, not lipstick, not perfume, not comb, not mirror, not stockings, not underwear, and not a beautiful dress.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I thought to myself, what is he's going to answer? And he said, you are right. You can't go without that to a theater. Tomorrow morning, you come to the door. I will be waiting for you. And I go out with you together, to fetch the things what you want. So next morning I thought he-- he forgotten. No. They said, Lageralteste Magda, come to the front. Here stands Kramer.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, but I need 50 people. He didn't ask for what. Have 50 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here we watched-- we was walking in the front, the commander and me, and 50 people behind. When we arrived to the place where they sorted out the goods which the arrivals brought. So he said, wait here. And I'm going to bring you everything, what you need. So I quickly, to the next person, I said, get yourself those trolleys. And pack them up, five trolleys. So that always, 10 girls could carry it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And pack them up with soap, medicaments, towels, blankets, jumpers, whatever. [INAUDIBLE] full, all. So quickly I said, get everybody to leave everywhere, to fill it because you won't be too long. They filled it so big, the 10 girls, to every one. And they were standing. Here the commander arrive. And he says, he bought a little cabinet which is a mirror, which can be moved this way and that way and had little two drawers. And we opened the drawers and here he said, here you have the soap, the perfume, the lipstick, there. Here you have the brush, the comb, the this, the that. Here you have the underwear, the stockings, the that. And here you have beautiful silver dress.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you satisfied? I said very. I want to carry it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I promised I get it for you. I get it. So here we again, go to the door, to the camp. And the girls with the trolleys behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he saw the girls with all the trolleys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course he saw them. So-- and I thought, what will happen now? He says to the SS man, let them through. And when they are empty, take them back with the girls, few girls, and take them back, where it comes from. And he said, and now, I give it to you. I brought it till the camp. Now you can take it to your room. And now you can go to the theater. But what we had, not what I got, this. But we had soap and medication and towels and jumpers and-- which we divided by-- I put, always, 50-- the-- the runners put 50 women. And they came in. And so slowly we divided all the things. And so many people had help-- soap and hygiene and medication. We left and that we gave to the doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- how many women were at that camp, in-- in--

speaker: interviewee
text: 32,000 women.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you were in charge over? 32,000?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: That you were in charge of? 32,000 women?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people did you have assisting you? 50?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have assistant? No. I made it differently. First of all, I established commandos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hygiene is very important. A woman came to me from my home town. I'm from your home town. Could you help me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, yes, you are going to help me. You pick hundred woman and make sure the cleanliness in the camp is spotless. The washrooms, the toilet, and everywhere. I picked somebody for runners. Because the camp was very [? wide. ?] If there is a message somewhere, there, I-- I-- I-- when I was picked to be lageralteste there, so I picked, again, some woman to be blockalteste for the 32 barracks from girls who was working outside the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So again, to save them. And they picked from the-- the people who arrived, people-- 16 people who helped them with the bread, deliveries also. And then I-- I sat with the runner to run around. And all the doctors should announce and come to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I divided them in every barrack, two or three accordingly. And I said to them, to watch if a epidemic breaks out. Then when there is, please come and tell me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not a long time it went. And the epidemic, scarlet fever, came out. And they said, what shall we do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, there, in the back of the barrack, is a wash house. Put them there. And the blockalteste was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: She asked and how will we with the role call? I said, that, leave to me. Because it was very hot. It was probably June. So this would be June, '40--

speaker: interviewer
text: June.

speaker: interviewee
text: Must be June.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hot. So I approach [? Grazer, ?] who was the-- the commander of this camp. I said, what should you run and do the counting? Because I supposed to go with her anyway. So I said, be here in the shade. I will do the counting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she said, what will the woman commander says from other camp? I said, are you the-- the commander of this camp? Yes. So what you care about-- do what is more comfortable for you. You stay here, and also the report writer. And I will bring the amount. We will compare. And if it's everything, all right, that's all right. So that's how I did for six weeks. Because that is the time, how long the scarlet fever last. And after that, I let her do that. But I went with her anyway. So-- What would have happened if you had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What would have happened if you hadn't done that?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I wouldn't done it and they would find out that scarlet fever is there, the epidemic there, they will take the 32,000 people to the crematoria. How do I know about that? Because opposite us was a small camp. They called that Mexico. They had 8,000 people, women there. Their style wasn't so thinking of the good of the people. And so they didn't do any precaution. And then scarlet fever broke out. And the SS found out. They ordered the tracks. They put the 8,000 women right to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- how-- how did you know that had happened, that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they was cross the road. And sometimes they needed something from the big camp, so they came over. And also, they was in touch with us because we knew those girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so who told you that, in the end? Was it one of the inmates? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the-- who was in function.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- who was in charge. She said, imagine what happened. I said, what happened? Scarlet fever broke out. They found out. And they took all the 8,000 people to the gas chamber. I didn't tell her you should-- you should prevent it. But I'm not here to preach others, what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did other people in the camp understand what you'd done?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody should know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even the other Jewish inmates of the camp shouldn't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only the doctors. And only the blockalteste. Because if one woman, just by off chance would say, we've been in the back of the barrack because we had scarlet fever. Well-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So everything has to be very secretive. Like, because you couldn't rely on-- maybe she will say something silly, not thinking about anything. And one thing I tell you, one day came a doctor to the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Dr. Klein. He was Romanian, origin. And he spoke Hungarian. And he came and he started to gather around himself some girls. They was young girls, beautiful girls. But everybody likes the comfort.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, why should you be here in that dirty place? And I have a beautiful sanatorium. You are beautiful girls. I take you there.

speaker: interviewee
text: There will be gardens and this and that. So the runners run. Magda, come quickly. Because they are-- they are gathering and they are pushing themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: They all wants to go to the sanatorium. So what can you do, quick? You have to be a little bit tough. So I said first, you are not going to go to the sanatorium. Because if I can be in this bloody camp, so could you to. And they still wanted to go. So I said, here you have sanatorium. Here you [? are. ?] And run, all of you, from here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to the doctor, Dr. Klein, you don't belong here. You have nothing to do here. If I will tell that to the commander Kramer that you've been here, in that camp, where you haven't got-- then you-- he won't-- won't be pleased.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you better move out. I could say that because he's not going to really ask Kramer. And also, I could afford because Kramer behaved to me differently like to others. He had a certain respect for me. Like what respect? One day, a runner comes and says, Magda, come to the front.

speaker: interviewee
text: The lagerkommandant wants you. I came to the front. He is sitting in a big, black car. And this is before the SS man are there, in the boot. And the lagerkommandant-- Irma Grese is there. And he says, lageralteste, come sit next to me in the car. And I said Lagerkommandant Kramer, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: How will it look that I, Jewish, sitting next to the commander? What will they think what connection we have together? Here, Dr. Mengele.

speaker: interviewee
text: You heard of Dr. Mengele. Here he is. Take him to the car. Let him bloody walk! And all the SS heard I want you to the car. I have to discuss with you some matters.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, you know what, [INAUDIBLE], I make a compromise. That car had a-- like a step in the front. Old cars had that. New cars hasn't got.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will stand on that Stand and I will lean on the car. And that's how we will go to the camp. What I forgot, the Warsaw Uprising, they heard about me. And they sent-- and I don't know how they did that, because it's dangerous-- they send me from the uprising a ring, a memorabilia for-- from the uprising. And I had that on my ring. And because it was so quickly run, because-- so I didn't think to take it off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as my hand was here on the car. And he said, what? You're wearing the ring of the Warsaw Uprising before my eyes? I take the revolver and shoot the whole hand off! And he said, no, you don't even move your hand away? I said, no. You are the commander. And you will do what you want anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He did like that. Oh, go away, he says. And he put the revolver in. And that all-- the commander and that-- all they saw, saw my prestige went higher. And so many things I could do which I wouldn't be able to if I wouldn't be so outspoken, daring. Because I said, only once I am going to the chimney. But till I go, I do everything in the good of the people. Even if it's dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened with that doctor that came, and you sent him away? Did he take any of the girls with him? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I would let him take one?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were his intentions? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: To send them to the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would he come specially there just to pick them out himself and send them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how do you know that that was his intention? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Because-- because he was-- he didn't have any sanatorium. He didn't have anything. He wasn't a top doctor. He was just [YIDDISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in charge of the 32,000 women?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about at-- September, let's say, about six months, eh?

speaker: interviewer
text: September '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: '44. The camp finished. And imagine, that shows you, the commander came to pick me up. And he said, come with me. Now I show you how hard you were going to work and how hard you will have it. And I said, like all other Jews. Now we will have it even harder. So it's my fate. I can't change it. So-- And he was teasing me that till we arrived to the kitchen in Birkenau. He came to the kitchen and there was the SS woman in charge. And she said-- and he said, here I bring you the kapo of the kitchen. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was thinking there is a kapo. Because you know everything beforehand, when I was in Birkenau. Kapo of the kitchen was a German woman, who was a lesbian friend of the lageralteste, of the German lageralteste.

speaker: interviewee
text: The German lageralteste was very good to the Jews and sympathetic. Now, if I take the position of her girlfriend, and she maybe have-- I don't know what position-- she will be bad to the Jews. And I don't need that at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, [? Schulz ?] was her name, [? Aufseherin ?] [? Schulz. ?] You know what? I have a proposition. I am much better in counting the food, like the mathematic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Counting the food, and I will be in the office. And let her stay a kapo. She is used to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what will the commander say? He will be satisfied. If you think that he will be satisfied, that's all right. So I got the-- in the office. And I was in charge to count out the food. How much that, and how much that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The prisoners didn't know why their rations was doubled. Because I now counting. I counted everything double. But I wasn't there too long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't there too long. Because suddenly, they called--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Magda to the front. So how long were you there? Weeks? Days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, four weeks, about.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it would--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe-- maybe not even four weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're looking at maybe--

speaker: interviewee
text: Two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are we looking at October? 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: October, that was the beginning of October. Say, in the middle of October.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I have to consider what happened. So again, the lagerkommandant, with the black car. And now I want you to sit in the car. So now I am going to take you somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Well, we'll hold it there and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?
